ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Japanese hambagu (hamburg steak) with cheese and spaghetti. FOODON_00002764 (hamburger steak food product) is the closest defensible match. The dish is a Japanese-style beef patty (hambagu) grilled and topped with melted cheese, served alongside spaghetti.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Beef meat preparations) is the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Japanese hambagu with cheese and spaghetti as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Japanese dish. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of grilled cheese beef hamburg spaghetti is approximately 350g, comprising a beef patty (~150g), melted cheese topping (~30g), spaghetti (~120g), and tomato-based sauce (~50g).

nutrient_source.title: 453D Grilled Cheese Beef Hamburg Spaghet — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '453D Grilled Cheese Beef Hamburg Spaghet' (cr_id: G00010981). All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef patty and cheese composite data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being spaghetti (cooked, GI ~46–58 per University of Sydney GI Database, mean ~52). The beef patty and cheese components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (8.85g/100g × 350g serving = 30.975g) / 100 = 16.1 for full serving. Note: GL per 100g portion = 4.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279mg per 100g — more than double the 600mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 350g serving delivers approximately 4477mg sodium, far exceeding the 2000mg daily recommended limit, and the combination of processed cheese, seasoned beef patty, and tomato sauce makes sodium reduction without recipe modification impractical.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from spaghetti component with low GI (~52); high fat content may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in a controlled small portion (100–150g) given the low GI of spaghetti (~52) and high protein content (16.1g/100g) which slows gastric emptying, but must account for the cumulative carbohydrate load from a full serving and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the high fat content (23.1g/100g) can delay and prolong the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5g/100g), high sodium (1279mg/100g), and cholesterol (78mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat content (~9.5g/100g from beef and cheese), very high sodium (1279mg/100g), and moderate dietary cholesterol (78mg/100g), all of which contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and blood pressure; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited to no more than 100g and frequency kept to occasional.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6), high saturated fat (~9.5g/100g), and nightshade tomato sauce are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as beef is a primary dietary trigger for inflammatory flares via arachidonic acid pathways, the estimated saturated fat (~9.5g/100g) exceeds RA dietary thresholds, and the tomato-based sauce contains nightshade compounds that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Cheese Beef Hamburg Spaghetti (Japanese: グリルドチーズビーフハンバーグスパゲッティ) is a Japanese-style Western fusion dish (yoshoku) consisting of a seasoned ground beef patty — known in Japan as hambagu (ハンバーグ) — that is grilled or pan-fried until cooked through, then topped with melted processed cheese and served alongside a portion of spaghetti dressed in a tomato-based sauce. The dish is a staple of Japanese family restaurants (famiresu) and Western-style Japanese diners, and has been adapted for the Singapore market. The beef patty is typically made from a mixture of ground beef, onion, egg, breadcrumbs, and seasoning, giving it a tender, juicy texture with a slightly caramelised exterior. The melted cheese adds richness and a creamy, salty contrast to the savoury beef. The spaghetti component is typically cooked al dente and coated in a mildly sweet tomato sauce. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100g, with high fat (23.1g/100g) and moderate protein (16.1g/100g). The sodium content is very high at 1279mg/100g, making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The calcium content is notably elevated at 1375mg/100g, likely reflecting the processed cheese component. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low) based on the spaghetti component. This dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis, and should be consumed in controlled portions by those managing Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010981) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for beef patty and cheese and is documented as an adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the spaghetti component.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for a composite of cooked ground beef patty (USDA FDC #174032) and processed American cheese (USDA FDC #171242) in proportions consistent with the dish description; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. Trans fat (0.1g/100g) is estimated from USDA FDC beef patty data; HPB does not report trans fat. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0g/100g per the HPB source; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, likely reflecting the refined spaghetti and processed ingredients used. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked spaghetti GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per 350g serving ≈ 16.1. 6. Calcium at 1375mg/100g is unusually high and is preserved verbatim from the HPB source; this likely reflects the processed cheese component and/or a data entry that includes a calcium-rich sauce or fortified ingredient. Users should verify this value against the original HPB record. 7. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine (yoshoku — Western-influenced Japanese cooking) sold in Singapore; multilingual naming follows the Japanese cuisine rule (Japanese script mandatory). 8. The food_name has been normalised from the HPB menu code '453D Grilled Cheese Beef Hamburg Spaghet' to the canonical English form 'Grilled Cheese Beef Hamburg Spaghetti'; the original HPB name is preserved in aliases.
